summary: DeVault argues that web analytics should be held to the same ethical standard of informed consent that governs research on human subjects in other fields. He outlines the three core elements of informed consent—disclosure, understanding, and voluntary agreement—and demonstrates that virtually no current web tracking practice meets any of them. He dismisses opt-out mechanisms, cookie pop-ups, and even newer 'pro-privacy' analytics tools as failing this standard. He welcomes GDPR enforcement as deserved comeuppance for an industry that has systematically exploited users, and warns that trying to find loopholes will not work because laws are interpreted by humans, not computers.
key_points: Web analytics should meet the same informed consent standards applied to human research in other fields: disclosure, understanding, and voluntary agreement
Common industry practices—opt-out toggles, cookie banners, implied consent—all fail to meet genuine informed consent
Even post-GDPR 'pro-privacy' analytics solutions don't truly qualify as ethical user tracking
Legal crackdowns like GDPR are a well-earned consequence of the industry's fundamental disrespect for users
If you want analytics data ethically, compensate users for their participation like any other research study
money_quotes: If your analytics script wouldn't pass muster at your university's ethics board, then what the hell is it doing on your website?
"By continuing to use this website, you agree to…" is not informed consent.
Our industry's fundamental failure to respect users, deliberately mining their data without consent and without oversight for profit, is the reason why we're seeing legal crackdowns.
Our comeuppance is well-earned, and I hope that the regulators give it teeth in enforcement.
The law is not a computer program, and it is not executed by computers: it is executed by human beings who can see through your horseshit.
You're going to stop spying on people.
key_insight: If web analytics wouldn't pass an ethics board's informed consent review, they have no business being deployed on users without their genuine, voluntary, and informed agreement.
